senses.definition: to buy the whole supply of something such as tickets, stocks, goods, etc.

examples.x: The company managed to buy up the limited edition items and create a buzz.
The concert promoter aimed to buy up all available tickets to guarantee a sold-out show.
The wealthy businessman plans to buy up real estate in the downtown area.
The collector decided to buy up rare stamps from various sources.
The store decided to buy up the seasonal items before they ran out.

senses.definition: to count all the money a shop made at the end of the day

examples.x: The cashier will cash up before closing the store.
Every evening, the manager has to cash up the till.
Don't forget to cash up at the end of your shift.
The employees collectively cash up at the end of each business day.
Let's cash up the sales for today.

senses.definition: to examine something to confirm its quality and accuracy

examples.x: The supervisor checked up on the team's work to maintain standards.
They checked up on the machinery to ensure it was functioning well.
The doctor checked up on the patient after the surgery.
Let's check up on the progress of the project this afternoon.
She routinely checks up on the quality of the products.

senses.definition: to add up a group of items or numbers to determine the total

examples.x: Children in the classroom were excited to count up the days remaining until the school trip.
During the inventory check, the team had to count up the available stock in the warehouse.
As the clock continued to tick, the audience watched the digital scoreboard count up the points in the intense basketball game.
The financial analyst used a spreadsheet to count up the quarterly profits and losses for the company.
She counted up the receipts to see how much they had spent.

senses.definition: to give someone the money one owes

examples.x: He borrowed money last month and needs to pay up by Friday.
The landlord demanded that the tenants pay up or face eviction.
It's the end of the month; time to pay up for the services we used.
They threatened to take legal action if he didn't pay up.
When the final reminder came in the mail, she realized she had to pay up immediately.

senses.definition: to dispose of all one's merchandise or possessions

examples.x: They decided to sell up their entire collection of rare stamps.
The store owner had to sell up the remaining stock after going out of business.
The retired couple decided to sell up their family home and downsize.
The estate sale allowed them to sell up the deceased person's belongings.
To fund their new venture, they had to sell up a lot of their assets.

senses.definition: to pay the money one owes someone

examples.x: It's important to settle up any outstanding dues before the due date.
The client agreed to settle up the invoice within the agreed-upon timeframe.
They promised to settle up the loan by the end of the month.
The client must settle up the invoice on time.
Pay and settle up before the due date.

senses.definition: to examine someone or something in order to form a judgment

examples.x: When buying a used car, it's wise to size up its condition and history before making a purchase.
As a coach, it's important to size up the strengths and weaknesses of each player on the team.
In a job interview, employers often size up candidates based on their qualifications and presentation.

senses.definition: to pay money, often unwillingly or under pressure

examples.x: After dinner, everyone had to stump up their share of the bill for the meal.
The unexpected repair costs meant that each tenant had to stump up additional funds for the maintenance of the building.
The group of friends decided to split the cost of the vacation, and each person had to stump up their portion in advance.
In order to secure their spots on the team, the players had to stump up a registration fee for the sports league.

senses.definition: to observe someone closely to evaluate their character, abilities, etc.

examples.x: The manager needs to weigh up the candidates before making a hiring decision.
The jury had to weigh up the evidence presented in court to reach a verdict.
The journalist spent weeks weighing up the credibility of her sources for the article.
The teacher is continuously weighing up the students' progress in the class.
